Multivariate Gaussian animal models

Source: vignettes/articles/multivariate.Rmd
multivariate.Rmd

Use the multivariate path when the same animals have two or more quantitative traits and the question is about genetic covariance: which traits share additive-genetic variation, what are the genetic correlations, and what are the trait-specific heritabilities?

This path is experimental and opt-in. It is useful for tiny validation-scale Gaussian examples, but it is not yet an ASReml-style production multi-trait claim and not yet supported by a committed known-truth recovery fixture. The examples are not run when this page is built because they require a local Julia, JuliaCall, and a sibling HSquared.jl checkout.

Model

For two traits measured on animal records, the fitted model is

vec(𝐚)∼MVN(𝟎,𝐆0⊗𝐀),vec(𝐞)∼MVN(𝟎,𝐑0⊗𝐈). \mathrm{vec}(\mathbf a) \sim \mathrm{MVN}\left(\mathbf 0, \mathbf G_0 \otimes \mathbf A\right), \qquad \mathrm{vec}(\mathbf e) \sim \mathrm{MVN}\left(\mathbf 0, \mathbf R_0 \otimes \mathbf I\right).

Here A is the pedigree additive relationship matrix, G0 is the trait-by-trait additive-genetic covariance matrix, and R0 is the trait-by-trait residual covariance matrix. hsquared sends an NA-preserving response matrix to HSquared.jl; missing response cells are masked by the engine. Missing fixed-effect values are still rejected before fitting.

Current syntax

The current live grammar uses a cbind() response and the ordinary animal-model random intercept:

fit_mv <- hsquared(
  cbind(weight, length) ~ sex + age + animal(1 | id, pedigree = ped),
  data = dat,
  family = gaussian(),
  REML = TRUE,
  control = hs_control(
    engine = "julia",
    engine_control = list(target = "multivariate")
  )
)

Read this as one additive animal effect shared across traits. The trait covariances live in G0; the residual covariances live in R0.

Missing trait cells

Trait cells may be missing in the cbind() response. For example, this is a valid data shape for the multivariate target:

dat <- data.frame(
  id = c("sire", "dam", "calf1", "calf2"),
  sex = c("m", "f", "f", "m"),
  age = c(4, 4, 1, 1),
  weight = c(72, 65, 31, 35),
  length = c(110, NA, 72, 76)
)

ped <- data.frame(
  id = c("sire", "dam", "calf1", "calf2"),
  sire = c(NA, NA, "sire", "sire"),
  dam = c(NA, NA, "dam", "dam")
)

fit_mv <- hsquared(
  cbind(weight, length) ~ sex + age + animal(1 | id, pedigree = ped),
  data = dat,
  family = gaussian(),
  REML = TRUE,
  control = hs_control(
    engine = "julia",
    engine_control = list(target = "multivariate")
  )
)

The missing length value is allowed because it is a response cell. A missing sex, age, or id value would be rejected because those variables define the fixed-effect and random-effect design matrices.

Extracting results

The multivariate fit returns the same kind of object as the univariate fit, plus trait covariance matrices:

genetic_covariance(fit_mv)
residual_covariance(fit_mv)

G_matrix(fit_mv)
R_matrix(fit_mv)

genetic_correlation(fit_mv)
residual_correlation(fit_mv)

heritability(fit_mv)
breeding_values(fit_mv)
fit_diagnostics(fit_mv)

heritability(fit_mv) is per trait. breeding_values(fit_mv) returns EBVs by animal and trait. fit_diagnostics(fit_mv) is the first place to look before interpreting likelihood-based summaries; logLik() and AIC() are intentionally blocked for non-converged multivariate fits.

Initial values

If you supply starting values, use a named list. The names are part of the bridge contract:

fit_mv <- hsquared(
  cbind(weight, length) ~ sex + age + animal(1 | id, pedigree = ped),
  data = dat,
  family = gaussian(),
  REML = TRUE,
  control = hs_control(
    engine = "julia",
    engine_control = list(
      target = "multivariate",
      initial = list(
        G0 = diag(1, 2),
        R0 = diag(1, 2)
      )
    )
  )
)

A bare two-element list is rejected because it is too easy to swap genetic and residual covariance starts by accident.

Planned structured grammar

The long-format structured covariance grammar is planned, not implemented:

# Planned, not fitted yet:
y ~ trait + sex + age +
  animal(trait | id, pedigree = ped, cov = us())

# Planned, not fitted yet:
y ~ trait + sex + age +
  animal(trait | id, pedigree = ped, cov = fa(K = 2))

Use the cbind() path above for the current opt-in multivariate model. The structured grammar is reserved for later unstructured, diagonal, low-rank, and factor-analytic covariance models.

Claim boundary

This page describes a partial capability:

  • fitted only through engine = "julia" and target = "multivariate";
  • Gaussian and REML-only;
  • animal-model-only;
  • dense validation-scale;
  • missing response cells allowed, missing fixed-effect cells rejected;
  • no cov = us(), cov = diag(), cov = lowrank(K), or cov = fa(K) grammar yet;
  • no external comparator or committed t >= 2 known-truth recovery claim yet.

Use validation_status() and the model-status article before treating any multivariate result as more than an experimental fit.
